children.author: grayhatter
children.textRawHtml:
> It doesn't matter whether you misunderstand LLM summary or LLM "misunderstands" you – there are real risks involved, and you wouldn't want them to weigh on your conscience if they were to materialize.

"fun" fact, a number (some? many? all?) of the LLM speech to text models will editorialize or hallucinate words for you, to make your speech fit the pattern it expects.

I don't think it's appropriate to use a model that can editorialize in a medical transcription setting. I feel that crosses a huge number of ethical lines.

> At the moment consensus is that it must not be banned, but also not mandated in any way - people must take responsibility, and they must be able to decide for themselves where and when the LLM use is justified and where it is not.

I have a really hard time understanding how supposed experts in their field, have been taken in so perfectly and so completely. You're a trained and certified medical/healthcare worker, responsible for the health, safety and well being of other humans, and yet willingly turn over parts of the process that exists to protect the safety and health of others, to a machine that you know can and will make mistakes you wouldn't or even couldn't ever reasonably make. You're supposed to make sure it works and is safe for everyone you're responsible for before you start using it... but then, I prefer healthcare that doesn't move fast, and break humans.

I'm sure someone will try to play devil's advocate and suggest it's a good thing to ration care, and that you have to acknowledge that you can't save everybody, faster is better after all But no, I'm angry enough about it that if a licensed person suggests that, I'll gladly complain to your licensing board (rhetorically speaking, I'm not gonna try to doxx anyone). You can't ethically argue for rationing care, and letting a machine known to make errors in ethics or care decisions, that you have been trained not to make. (Advocating for a reduction in the standard of care in order to increase profits.) (Yes, you're correct, if you're in a state of triage, you're required to, and in those cases, using an imperfect machine is possibly preferable, and probably ethical, but still objectionable. I currently refuse to believe that the US is in a state of emergency, but perhaps that's where I'm wrong) Part of your licensure you promised to prioritize patient care above personal gain. You can't then make decisions to experiment with systems you cant prove are safe.

I can understand in when SWEs ignore the best interests of the humans they're working for. But Doctors and Nurses? I guess I expected better from my former colleagues?
children.textZhHtml:
> 不管你是误解了LLM摘要还是LLM“误解”了你——都存在真正的风险，如果它们成为现实，你也不希望它们让你的良心感到沉重。

“有趣”的事实是，一些（一些？很多？全部？）LLM演讲到文本模型会为你编辑或幻化单词，让你的演讲符合它的模式。期望。

我认为使用可以在医学转录环境中进行编辑的模型是不合适的。我觉得这跨越了很多道德界限。

>目前的共识是，它不能被禁止，但也不能以任何方式强制 - 人们必须承担责任，他们必须能够自己决定何时何地使用法学硕士是合理的，哪里不合理。

我真的很难理解他们所在领域的所谓专家是如何被如此完美和彻底地接受的。您是一名经过培训和认证的医疗/保健工作者，对其他人类的健康、安全和福祉负责，但愿意将保护他人安全和健康的部分流程移交给一台您知道可以而且将会犯您不会甚至永远不可能合理犯的错误的机器。你应该做在你开始使用它之前，确保它有效并且对你负责的每个人都是安全的...但是，我更喜欢那些行动不快、会伤害人类的医疗保健。

我确信有人会试图唱反调，建议配给护理是一件好事，而且你必须承认你无法拯救所有人，更快更好。但是不，我对此很生气，如果有执照的人提出这样的建议，我很乐意向你的执照委员会投诉（从言辞上来说，我不会尝试对任何人进行人肉搜索）。你不能在道德上主张配给护理，并让机器知道在道德或护理决策上犯错误，而你已经被训练不要犯这些错误。 （主张降低护理标准以增加利润。）（是的，你是对的，如果你处于分诊状态，你就需要这样做，在这种情况下，使用不完美的机器可能更可取，而且可能是道德的，但仍然令人反感。我目前拒绝相信美国处于紧急状态，但也许这就是我错的地方）在你的许可中，你承诺将患者护理置于个人利益之上。然后，您就无法决定对无法证明安全的系统进行实验。

当 SWE 忽视他们所服务的人类的最大利益时，我可以理解。但是医生和护士呢？我想我对以前的同事期望更好？

children.author: nradov
children.textRawHtml:
In a medical setting we first have to be clear about whether the LLM is acting as a transcriptionist or a scribe. Those are different roles. A transcriptionist merely writes down everything the clinician says verbatim, with perhaps a bit of formatting to fit a standard template. Speech recognition software has been used for transcription since before LLMs even existed. Sometimes a human transcriptionist will review and edit the software output. But ultimately the clinician is accountable for approving the document before it legally becomes part of the patient chart.

A medical scribe isn't just taking dictation, they're doing some level of interpretation and often entering structured, coded data directly into an EHR. This is a more complex role and requires some clinical skills. There are some new LLM products that automate this to an extent, but ultimately the licensed clinician is still legally accountable for what goes into the patient chart.

I can absolutely ethically argue for rationing care. All healthcare systems ration care although the means vary. Demand is effectively infinite, especially from older patients with complex or terminal conditions. Resources are finite.
children.textZhHtml:
在医疗环境中，我们首先必须明确法学硕士是充当转录员还是抄写员。这些是不同的角色。转录员只是逐字写下临床医生所说的所有内容，并可能进行一些格式化以适应标准模板。早在法学硕士存在之前，语音识别软件就已被用于转录。有时，人工转录员会审查和编辑软件输出。但最终，临床医生有责任在文件合法地成为患者病历的一部分之前批准该文件。

医疗抄写员不仅进行听写，还进行一定程度的解释，并经常将结构化的编码数据直接输入 EHR。这是一个更复杂的角色，需要一些临床技能。有一些新的法学硕士产品可以在一定程度上实现这一过程的自动化，但最终持证临床医生仍然对患者图表中的内容负有法律责任。

我绝对可以在道德上主张配给护理。尽管手段各不相同，但所有医疗保健系统都实行配给护理。需求实际上是无限的，尤其是患有复杂或绝症的老年患者。资源是有限的。

children.author: phoghed
children.textRawHtml:
> I have a really hard time understanding how supposed experts in their field, have been taken in so perfectly and so completely.

Yes, surely it’s not you. It’s all the experts that are wrong and being hoodwinked. Why can’t they just see what you can?
children.textZhHtml:
> 我真的很难理解他们所在领域的所谓专家是如何被如此完美和彻底地接受的。

是的，当然不是你。都是专家错了，被蒙蔽了。为什么他们不能看到你能看到的东西？

children.author: grayhatter
children.textRawHtml:
> Why can’t they just see what you can?

Most humans use a default trust model for life; they've been lied to. That's part of why I'm angry, if you trust other people; why would you look for it? This entire article is about the fallout from not looking for it, and the resulting harms to pt care. So, it turns out, it was a mistake to adopt the new tech quickly; you know, as evidenced by the harms to patients?

So I don't know, why do you think they couldn't they predict the harms that I thought were obviously predictable, and then happened?
children.textZhHtml:
> 为什么他们不能直接看到你能看到的东西？

大多数人在生活中都采用默认的信任模式；他们被骗了。这也是我生气的部分原因，如果你信任别人，你为什么要去怀疑呢？整篇文章讲的就是不去怀疑所带来的后果，以及由此对患者护理造成的伤害。所以，事实证明，快速采用新技术是个错误；你知道的，这从对患者造成的伤害就能看出来？

所以我不知道，你觉得他们为什么没能预测到那些我认为显而易见、且最终确实发生了的危害呢？

children.author: red75prime
children.textRawHtml:
> where and when the LLM use is justified and where it is not

while being bombarded with articles like "AI makes things worse", "AI consumes all the water" and the like
children.textZhHtml:
> 何时何地使用 LLM 是合理的，何时不合理

同时受到诸如“AI 让事情变得更糟”、“AI 消耗所有水”等文章的轰炸

children.author: isityettime
children.textRawHtml:
Things like this are (sadly) common (and age-old) problems with automation and computerization. (For a vivid account of this phenomenon, check out the novel _Close to the Machine_, by Ellen Ullman.)

As executives and analysts increasingly use the "AI" craze to push automation and computerization (and layoffs) generally, even aside from AI proper, it should not be surprising that the individuals and groups opposing those moves also use the same labels.

The lack of precision in language here sucks. It sucks for the discourse and it also sucks when it comes to focusing anger and productive energy on the core problems (obfuscation of human responsibility, erosion of human agency, declining institutional flexibility, deprofessionalization, etc.). But it doesn't begin with the critics of AI.
children.textZhHtml:
类似的事情（可悲的是）是自动化和计算机化的常见（而且是古老的）问题。 （要了解这一现象的生动描述，请阅读 Ellen Ullman 的小说《靠近机器》。）

随着高管和分析师越来越多地利用“AI”热潮来推动自动化和计算机化（以及裁员）普遍，即使除了 AI 本身之外，那些反对这些举措的个人和团体也使用相同的标签也就不足为奇了。

这里的语言缺乏精确性很糟糕。它不适合讨论，也不适合将愤怒和生产能量集中在核心问题上（人类责任的模糊、人类能动性的侵蚀、制度灵活性的下降、非专业化等）。但这并不是从人工智能的批评开始的。

children.author: johnnyanmac
children.textRawHtml:
>The lack of precision in language here sucks.

It's a feature. Or at least, a perk. If they want to claim this new shiny rock is AI and people buy it, then of course it's in their best interest to keep the black box mysterious. Being subterfuge for muddying the discourse of critique is just a nice side bonus.
children.textZhHtml:
>这里的语言缺乏精确性很糟糕。

这是一个功能。或者至少是一种福利。如果他们想声称这个新的闪亮岩石是人工智能并且人们购买它，那么当然保持黑匣子神秘才符合他们的最大利益。混淆批评话语的托词只是一个不错的附带好处。

children.author: HappMacDonald
children.textRawHtml:
But it is only a perk for the scam artists who benefit from that.

Yes, it makes sense that the confusion aligns with their interests, and they are unavoidably a big part of the conversation. But it remains a problem for the non-overlapping group of people who actually value the social contract, and for us finding a solution which helps take one more step to defeat the scammers remains valuable.
children.textZhHtml:
但这对于从中受益的骗子来说只是一种福利。

是的，这种混乱符合他们的兴趣，这是有道理的，而且他们不可避免地成为谈话的重要组成部分。但对于真正重视社会契约的非重叠群体来说，这仍然是一个问题，而对于我们来说，找到一种有助于进一步击败骗子的解决方案仍然很有价值。

children.author: delusional
children.textRawHtml:
I am very interested in your reading of Close to the Machine. I read it myself a couple of years ago and found it a wonderful telling of the early days of tech, with overtones of the "technology workplace" that were still very true to this day. I did not pick up on any commentary on automation or computerization, outside of the general critique of bureaucratic systems that alienate you from the outcomes of your labor.

Do you have anything I could read to understand your reading better? I would love to be able to dive back into one of my favorite books with a new lead.
children.textZhHtml:
我对您阅读《靠近机器》非常感兴趣。几年前，我自己读过这本书，发现它精彩地讲述了科技的早期发展，其中带有“科技工作场所”的暗示，至今仍然非常真实。除了对官僚系统的一般批评之外，我没有收到任何关于自动化或计算机化的评论，这些系统使你与劳动成果疏远。

你有什么我可以读的东西来更好地理解你的阅读内容吗？我很高兴能够以新的线索重新深入研究我最喜欢的书之一。

children.author: fn-mote
children.textRawHtml:
> AI-powered alerts from their apple watches that detected cardiac events

Surely these are “good old-fashioned AI” (statistical learning) and not LLM, though.

I just want to be clear that the “medical LLM” tools are the new ones, and the Apple Watch alerts aren’t.
children.textZhHtml:
> 来自苹果手表的人工智能警报检测到心脏事件

当然，这些是“好的老式人工智能”（统计学习），而不是法学硕士。

我只是想澄清一下，“医学法学硕士”工具是新工具，而 Apple Watch 警报不是。

children.author: bitwize
children.textRawHtml: LLMs are statistical learning. GOFAI is symbolic, rules-based stuff, expert systems and that.
children.textZhHtml: 法学硕士是统计学习。 GOFAI 是象征性的、基于规则的东西、专家系统等等。

children.author: kennywinker
children.textRawHtml: There is still a categorical difference between how they are being used. Specifically analytic vs generative. Generative AI (LLMs and image generators) are the ones people have issues with - pretty much nobody cares about ML processing for analysis.
children.textZhHtml: 它们的使用方式仍然存在本质上的区别。具体来说是分析型与生成型的区别。人们对生成式AI（大语言模型和图像生成器）颇有微词——而几乎没人在乎用于分析的机器学习处理。

stories.title: Microsoft Comic Chat is now open source
stories.titleZh: 微软漫画聊天现已开源

comments.author: outintospace
comments.textRawHtml:
Hi, I'm Robert Standefer, the guy who made this happen, with lots of support. I'm excited to see the enthusiasm about Comic Chat being open sourced. How this came to happen is a very interesting story that spans a six-year period with success that hinged upon being in the right place at the right time, literally.

I want to point out that, while I (along with Scott Hanselman) made the Comic Chat open source release happen, I am not the original developer. That is DJ Kurlander, and he was very supportive of this project. He was even enthusiastic about it.
comments.textZhHtml:
大家好，我是Robert Standefer，在大家的大力支持下，是我促成了这件事。看到大家对Comic Chat开源如此热情，我感到非常激动。这背后的故事非常有趣，跨越了六年的时间，而成功的关键确实在于“在对的时间出现在对的地点”。

我想说明的是，虽然是我（和Scott Hanselman一起）促成了Comic Chat的开源发布，但我并不是最初的开发者。最初的开发者是DJ Kurlander，他对这个项目非常支持，甚至可以说是充满热情。

children.author: 9dev
children.textRawHtml:
I take that comic a bit personal, to be honest - I just returned from a two-month odyssey to find a way of offering our customers a simple way of connecting our MCP Server to their Copilot Chat. Digging through a mindboggling number of documentation that was often outdated, sometimes contradictory, but always written both verbose and yet very light on information was part of that; talking to broken AI chatbots and clueless support staff was, just as trying out four (four!) different ways to create an Agent wrapper - only to discover that multi-tenancy is not supported for any of these, and two of the three SDKs are outdated but still referenced in docs everywhere.

What's more, people seemed to be actively confused by the use case ("Why would your customers even want to use an external tool that isn't part of their Microsoft environment?").

I finally found out about "declarative agents", which seem to be able to do what I need. And if I don't trash my computer against the wall out of pure rage over page changes in partner center taking 15 seconds or longer, I might just be able to complete the 40-step form required for the marketplace listing. Progress!
children.textZhHtml:
说实话，那部漫画让我觉得有点针对个人——我刚结束了一场为期两个月的“奥德赛”之旅，只为给我们的客户找到一种简单的方法，将我们的MCP服务器连接到他们的Copilot Chat。这其中包括翻阅海量文档，这些文档往往过时、有时自相矛盾，而且总是写得冗长却言之无物；还包括与故障频出的AI聊天机器人和一头雾水的客服人员沟通，以及尝试了四（四！）种不同的方法来创建Agent包装器——结果却发现这些方法全都不支持多租户，而且三个SDK中有两个已经过时，却仍在各处文档中被引用。

更让人无语的是，大家似乎对我们的用例感到十分困惑（“你们的客户为什么会想用一个不属于他们微软环境的外部工具？”）。

我终于发现了“声明式代理”，它似乎能满足我的需求。如果我没有因为合作伙伴中心页面加载需要15秒甚至更久而气得把电脑砸向墙壁的话，我说不定真能填完应用市场上架所需的那40步表单。有进展了！

children.author: csans-cstrip
children.textRawHtml:
Thanks for this. I love the work that individuals have been putting into open sourcing historical Microsoft products.

I notice there are two (identical) copies of the Comic Sans .ttf in the repo; are they also covered by the terms of the MIT license?

According to “otfinfo -i”, they’re an older version than the one typically packaged on Linux (https://sourceforge.net/projects/corefonts/files/the%20fonts...). Do you think you could swing an official release of the latest and/or historical versions of Comic Sans under MIT or another appropriate license like SIL OFL?
children.textZhHtml:
非常感谢。我非常欣赏个人开发者在将微软历史产品开源方面所付出的努力。

我注意到代码库里有两份（完全相同的）Comic Sans .ttf文件；它们也受MIT许可证条款的约束吗？

根据“otfinfo -i”的信息，它们比Linux上通常打包的版本要旧（https://sourceforge.net/projects/corefonts/files/the%20fonts...）。您觉得能否推动在MIT或其他合适的许可证（如SIL OFL）下官方发布最新版和/或历史版本的Comic Sans呢？

children.author: resonanttoe
children.textRawHtml:
Really nice work! This is so very very cool.

Comic chat was a phenomenal pain in the arse in the 90s for any IRC channel :P But it's still such a great and important piece of IRC history as a whole. It's amazing cool to see it open sourced and preserved this way.

It's amazing to see people do a tonne of work to get things like this and 3D Movie Maker - open sourced. I'd never thought I'd see the day!
children.textZhHtml:
干得漂亮！这简直太酷了。

在90年代，Comic Chat对任何IRC频道来说都真是个让人头疼的麻烦：P 但总体而言，它仍然是IRC历史中非常伟大且重要的一部分。看到它以这种方式开源并保存下来，真是太棒了。

看到大家付出大量努力让这类软件和3D Movie Maker开源，真是令人惊叹。我做梦也没想到会有这一天！
